It lies 89 km (55 mi) by road southwest of the centre of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") on the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"),[[3]](#cite_note-GM-3) and is part of [Eurometropolis Lille–Kortrijk–Tournai](/wiki/Eurometropolis_Lille%E2%80%93Kortrijk%E2%80%93Tournai "Eurometropolis Lille–Kortrijk–Tournai"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 2022, the municipality of Tournai had an estimated population of 68,518 people.[[5]](#cite_note-cityde-5)

**Tournai** ([/tʊərˈneɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*toor-NAY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), French: [[tuʁnɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Madehub-Tournai.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Madehub-Tournai.wav"); [Picard](/wiki/Picard_language "Picard language"): *Tornai*; [Walloon](/wiki/Walloon_language "Walloon language"): *Tornè* [[tɔʀnɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Walloon "Help:IPA/Walloon") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Wa_pcd_Torn%C3%A8.ogg "File:Wa pcd Tornè.ogg"); [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Doornik* [[ˈdoːrnɪk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Doornik.ogg "File:Nl-Doornik.ogg"), sometimes [anglicised](/wiki/Anglicisation_(linguistics) "Anglicisation (linguistics)") in older sources as "Tournay") is a city and [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_in_Belgium "Municipalities in Belgium") of [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") located in the [Province of Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_Province "Hainaut Province"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). It lies 89 km (55 mi) by road southwest of the centre of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") on the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"),[[3]](#cite_note-GM-3) and is part of [Eurometropolis Lille–Kortrijk–Tournai](/wiki/Eurometropolis_Lille%E2%80%93Kortrijk%E2%80%93Tournai "Eurometropolis Lille–Kortrijk–Tournai"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 2022, the municipality of Tournai had an estimated population of 68,518 people.[[5]](#cite_note-cityde-5)

Tournai is one of the oldest cities in Belgium and has played an important role in the country's cultural history. It was the first capital of the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Francia "Francia"), with [Clovis I](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") being born here.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tournai&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

Tournai lies 89 km (55 mi) by road southwest of the centre of [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") on the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt").[[3]](#cite_note-GM-3) Administratively, the town and municipality is part of the [Province of Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_Province "Hainaut Province"), in the [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") region of southwest Belgium.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The municipality has an area of 213.75 km2 (82.53 sq mi).[[5]](#cite_note-cityde-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tournai&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Tournai, known as *Tornacum*, was a place of minor importance in [Roman times](/wiki/Roman_times "Roman times"), a stopping place where the [Roman road](/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road") from [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") on the Rhine to [Boulogne](/wiki/Boulogne-sur-Mer "Boulogne-sur-Mer") on the coast crossed the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"). It was fortified under Emperor [Maximian](/wiki/Maximian "Maximian") in the 3rd century AD,[[7]](#cite_note-7) when the Roman *[limes](/wiki/Limes_(Roman_Empire) "Limes (Roman Empire)")* was withdrawn to the string of outposts along the road. It came into the possession of the [Salian Franks](/wiki/Salian_Franks "Salian Franks") in 432. Under King [Childeric I](/wiki/Childeric_I "Childeric I"), whose tomb was discovered there in 1653,[[8]](#cite_note-8) Tournai was the capital of the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Francia "Francia"). In 486, [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") moved the center of power to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"). In turn, a native son of Tournai, [Eleutherius](/wiki/Eleutherius_of_Tournai "Eleutherius of Tournai"), became bishop of the newly created [bishopric of Tournai](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Tournai "Bishopric of Tournai"), extending over most of the area west of the Scheldt. In 862, [Charles the Bald](/wiki/Charles_the_Bald "Charles the Bald"), first king of [Western Francia](/wiki/Western_Francia "Western Francia") and still to become [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"), would make Tournai the seat of the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Doornik_1581.jpg/250px-Doornik_1581.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doornik_1581.jpg)

Siege of Tournai, 1581

After the partition of the Frankish Empire by the Treaties of [Verdun (843)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Verdun "Treaty of Verdun") and of [Meerssen (870)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Meerssen "Treaty of Meerssen"), Tournai remained in the western part of the empire, which in 987 became France. The city participated in 11th-century rise of towns in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), with a woollen cloth industry based on English wool, which soon made it attractive to wealthy merchants. An ambitious rebuilding of the cathedral was initiated in 1030. [Odo of Orléans](/wiki/Odo_of_Tournai "Odo of Tournai") was appointed at the cathedral school of Tournai in 1087.[[9]](#cite_note-Stone-9) Under Odo's leadership, [Saint-Martin Abbey](/wiki/Saint-Martin_Abbey,_Tournai "Saint-Martin Abbey, Tournai") flourished and by 1105 had 70 monks.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The commune's drive for independence from the local counts succeeded in 1187, and the city was henceforth directly subordinated to the French Crown, as the *[seigneurie de Tournaisis](/wiki/Tournaisis "Tournaisis")*, as the city's environs are called. The stone [Bridge of the Holes](/w/index.php?title=Bridge_of_the_Holes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bridge of the Holes (page does not exist)") [[da](https://da.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pont_des_Trous "da:Pont des Trous"); [fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pont_des_Trous "fr:Pont des Trous"); [it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pont_des_Trous "it:Pont des Trous"); [nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pont_des_Trous "nl:Pont des Trous"); [pcd](https://pcd.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peont_des_Treos "pcd:Peont des Treos")] over the Scheldt, with defensive towers at either end, was built in 1290, replacing an earlier wooden structure.

In 1340, as a part of the Hundred Years' War, [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III") of England gathered a large army and [besieged](/wiki/Siege_of_Tournai_(1340) "Siege of Tournai (1340)") Tournai for a month. The operation was unsuccessful, bankrupting Edward and forcing him to sign the [Truce of Espléchin](/wiki/Truce_of_Espl%C3%A9chin "Truce of Espléchin").

During the 15th century, the city's textile trade boomed and it became an important supplier of [tapestry](/wiki/Tapestry "Tapestry"). The art of painting flourished too: [Jacques Daret](/wiki/Jacques_Daret "Jacques Daret"), [Robert Campin](/wiki/Robert_Campin "Robert Campin") and [Rogier van der Weyden](/wiki/Rogier_van_der_Weyden "Rogier van der Weyden") all came from Tournai. It was [captured in 1513](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Spurs "Battle of the Spurs") by [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") of England, making it the only Belgian city ever to have been ruled by England. It was also [represented](/wiki/Tournai_(Parliament_of_England_constituency) "Tournai (Parliament of England constituency)") in the 1515 [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The city was handed back to French rule in 1519, following the [Treaty of London (1518)](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1518) "Treaty of London (1518)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Eugene-Henry-Fricx-Table-des-cartes-des-Pays-Bas_MG_0564.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Eugene-Henry-Fricx-Table-des-cartes-des-Pays-Bas_MG_0564.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eugene-Henry-Fricx-Table-des-cartes-des-Pays-Bas_MG_0564.tif)

Siege of Tournai, 1709

In 1521, [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") added the city to his possessions in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), leading to a period of religious strife and economic decline. During the 16th century, Tournai was a bulwark of [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), but eventually it was conquered by the Spanish governor of the Low Countries, the [Duke of Parma](/wiki/Alessandro_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Alessandro Farnese, Duke of Parma and Piacenza"), following a prolonged siege in 1581.[[12]](#cite_note-12) After the fall of the city, its [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") inhabitants were given one year to sell their possessions and emigrate, a policy that was at the time considered relatively humane, since very often religious opponents were simply massacred.

One century later, in 1668, the city briefly returned to France under King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") in the [Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1668) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1668)") following the [siege of Tournai](/wiki/Siege_of_Tournai_(1667) "Siege of Tournai (1667)"). The city was [besieged](/wiki/Siege_of_Tournai_(1709) "Siege of Tournai (1709)") by the [Duke of Marlborough](/wiki/John_Churchill,_1st_Duke_of_Marlborough "John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough") during the [War of Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_Spanish_Succession "War of Spanish Succession") in 1709. At the end of the war in 1713, under terms of the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") the former [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), including Tournai, came into possession of the [Austrian Habsburgs](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands"). The city was again successfully [besieged](/wiki/Siege_of_Tournai_(1745) "Siege of Tournai (1745)") by France in 1745. In 1794, France annexed the [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands") during the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and Tournai became part of the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Jemmape](/wiki/Jemmape_(department) "Jemmape (department)"). From 1815 on, following the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), Tournai formed part of the [United Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") and after 1830 of [newly independent Belgium](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution"). Badly damaged in 1940 during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Tournai has since been carefully restored.

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tournai&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Main sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Tournai_JPG05a.jpg/250px-Tournai_JPG05a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tournai_JPG05a.jpg)

[Pont des Trous](/w/index.php?title=Pont_des_Trous&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pont des Trous (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pont_des_Trous "fr:Pont des Trous")] bridge on the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") with [Our Lady's Cathedral of Tournai](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral") in the distance

Tournai is considered to be one of the most important cultural sites in Belgium. The mixed [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture")- and [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture")-style [Cathedral of Our Lady](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral") and the city's [Belfry](/wiki/Belfry_of_Tournai "Belfry of Tournai"), considered the oldest in Belgium,[[13]](#cite_note-13) have been designated by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Inside the cathedral, the *Châsse de Notre-Dame flamande*, a beautifully ornate 12th-century [reliquary](/wiki/Reliquary "Reliquary"), gives witness to Tournai's wealth in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").

Other places of interest are the 13th-century [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") bridge (Pont des Trous)[[16]](#cite_note-16) and the main square ([Grand-Place](/wiki/Grand-Place_(Tournai) "Grand-Place (Tournai)")), as well as several old city gates, historic warehouses, and a variety of museums. As in many Belgian cities, there are a number of cafés and pubs on the Grand-Place. In the middle of the square, there are a series of water fountains, while a circular staircase to the top of the Belfry can be climbed.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On the Rue Barre-St-Brice are two of the oldest private houses in Europe, dating from between 1175 and 1200 and built in the Romanesque style,[[19]](#cite_note-19) while the Rue des Jésuites includes a Gothic house from the 13th century. There are also several buildings in the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") style across the city.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tournai&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Tournai%2C_la_Grand_Place_IMG_6461_2023-06-05_08.56.jpg/250px-Tournai%2C_la_Grand_Place_IMG_6461_2023-06-05_08.56.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tournai,_la_Grand_Place_IMG_6461_2023-06-05_08.56.jpg)

[Grand-Place of Tournai](/wiki/Grand-Place_(Tournai) "Grand-Place (Tournai)")

The dominant language in Tournai is French. The local language was historically *tournaisien*, a [Picard](/wiki/Picard_language "Picard language") dialect similar to that of other municipalities of Hainaut and Northern France. Tournai also belongs to [Romance Flanders](/wiki/Romance_Flanders "Romance Flanders"), like [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"), [Douai](/wiki/Douai "Douai"), [Tourcoing](/wiki/Tourcoing "Tourcoing"), and [Mouscron](/wiki/Mouscron "Mouscron"). The city was one of the greatest cultural and economic centres of the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders"). Some traces can still be seen today:

* The [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir") of [Our Lady's Cathedral](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral") is a precursory element of the Scaldian (meaning from the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") area), typically Flemish, [Gothic art](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art").
* The [Bishopric of Tournai](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Tournai "Roman Catholic Diocese of Tournai") was the religious capital of Flanders during more than a millennium (from 496 to 1559).
* The tapestries and draperies of Tournai belong to the great Flemish school of [tapestry](/wiki/Tapestry "Tapestry") and Tournai was part of the Flemish [Hansa](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") of London, which also included the draper towns of Flanders.
* The Church of St. Brice, dedicated to [Saint Britius](/wiki/Saint_Britius "Saint Britius"), is one of the first examples of the *hallekerk* style, so typical of the Flemish countryside.
* The [Church of St. Quentin](/wiki/Church_of_Saint_Quentin,_Tournai "Church of Saint Quentin, Tournai"), a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") in [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") style with [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") elements, known to have existed since the 10th century. The current building was built around 1200, but has been altered several times throughout history. It contains important sculptures by the 15th-century sculptor [Jean Delemer](/wiki/Jean_Delemer "Jean Delemer").
* Some of the great [Flemish Primitives](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting") are from Tournai: [Robert Campin](/wiki/Robert_Campin "Robert Campin"), [Rogier van der Weyden](/wiki/Rogier_van_der_Weyden "Rogier van der Weyden"), and [Jacques Daret](/wiki/Jacques_Daret "Jacques Daret").

Although Tournai is in the Flemish cultural area (of the Scheldt), it also possesses some treasures of the [Mosan style](/wiki/Mosan_art "Mosan art"). Indeed, the two most beautiful shrines of the cathedral, commissioned by the Bishop of Tournai, were made in the region of [Liège](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Bishopric of Liège") by the artist [Nicholas of Verdun](/wiki/Nicholas_of_Verdun "Nicholas of Verdun"): the shrines of *Saint-Eleutherius* and of *Our Lady of Flanders* (13th century). Those shrines testify to the opulence of Tournai and Liège during the Middle Ages. The shrine of *Our Lady of Flanders* has been called one of the seven wonders of Belgium.

### Festivities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tournai&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Festivities")]

* The "Great Procession" (in [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Grande Procession*) is a procession initiated by the bishop Radbot II during a plague epidemic. It has taken place every year since 1092, with the single exception of the year 1566, when the [iconoclasts](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") considerably damaged the religious symbols of the city. This historic [procession](/wiki/Procession "Procession") unfolds in the streets every second Sunday of September.
* The first Monday after January 6 is known as "Lost Monday" (in [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Lundi perdu*) or "Perjury Monday" (*Lundi parjuré*). This tradition dates from more than 700 years ago. The wealthier city inhabitants used to prepare fastidious family dinners and elect a king. Today, the family dinners have expanded to wider groups and a rabbit dish is often served.
